narrative: I'm sorry to share this tough news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 94% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on key details from your profile, like your academic decile ranks from Year 1 and Year 3, your LSAT score of 28, undergraduate GPA of 2.3, middle family income bracket, being Hispanic, female, and planning full-time studies. Compared to typical students in the dataset—who average around the 5th or 6th decile for those early years, an LSAT near 37, and a GPA over 3.2—your standings in school deciles were in the bottom ranges, putting you well below the norm. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 decile rank, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which further tipped the scales in the same direction, followed closely by your Year 1 decile. Your family income level, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender rounded out the influences, each contributing toward the failure prediction in decreasing order of impact, though none countered the overall trend. In essence, these factors together painted a picture riskier than what the model sees in students who typically pass the bar. While demographics like gender, race, and family income are harder to shift and weren't the top drivers, your academic markers stood out as the heaviest weights. To improve your future chances, focus on bolstering those changeable academic areas—consider retaking the LSAT with targeted prep to aim higher than 28, as it was a major factor. Building stronger study habits or seeking tutoring could help mimic the profiles of top performers in school deciles. Programs supporting underrepresented students like yourself might offer bar prep resources to address these gaps. With dedication to these steps, you could reshape your profile for a stronger prediction next time. Keep pushing forward—you have the potential to beat these odds.
